title: Trump Greenlights Saudi Uranium Enrichment as US Threatens Iran Nuclear Site, Houthi Blockade Forces Saudi Tanker Reversals
subhead: The Trump administration approved a 30-year nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia that could allow domestic uranium enrichment — without IAEA's strongest safeguards — on the same day Trump vowed to strike Iran's fortified Pickaxe Mountain facility. Meanwhile, Houthi threats against Red Sea shipping forced Saudi crude tankers to reverse course, and Russia hit a Ukraine-bound LPG tanker in Romanian waters.

sections.heading: Trump-Saudi Nuclear Deal Bypasses Proliferation Safeguards
sections.body:
President Trump has approved a 30-year nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia that could allow the kingdom to enrich uranium on its own soil, according to two sources familiar with the matter cited by the Associated Press and Reuters. The deal, expected to be announced publicly as early as Wednesday and submitted to Congress for review, would involve U.S. firms in developing Saudi Arabia's civilian nuclear program.

The agreement notably does not include the IAEA's Additional Protocol, which allows for enhanced monitoring, inspections, and verification beyond standard safeguards, according to one of the AP's sources. The absence of this provision has alarmed nonproliferation advocates, who warn it opens the door to a new round of nuclear competition in the Middle East — particularly striking given that the U.S. launched its war against Iran in part to dismantle Tehran's nuclear enrichment capability. The Wall Street Journal first reported the deal, and News18 estimated it to be worth tens of billions of dollars.

The deal would permit a joint U.S.-Saudi study on building a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia. Saudi Arabia is already a member of the IAEA, but the Additional Protocol is the mechanism that provides the intrusive inspections regime the U.S. has long insisted other nations adopt.
sections.whyItMatters: Approving uranium enrichment for Saudi Arabia without the IAEA's strongest inspection framework undermines the very nonproliferation logic the U.S. used to justify strikes on Iran. Gulf neighbors and nonproliferation advocates will see this as a strategic reward for alignment, not a genuine civilian energy program.

sections.heading: Trump Vows Strike on Iran's Pickaxe Mountain Nuclear Site
sections.body:
President Trump stated on Tuesday that the U.S. will strike Iran's Pickaxe Mountain, a heavily fortified underground facility suspected of housing nuclear activity, 'probably pretty soon.' The site, described as deep underground and difficult to penetrate even with bunker-buster munitions, represents a significant escalation target in the U.S.-Iran conflict.

The threat came as the U.S. military completed its 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran, according to U.S. Central Command, targeting Iranian military command centers, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defense systems. Explosions were reported in Tehran, Bushehr, Chabahar, and Konarak, while Iran activated air defenses around the capital. Iran's IRGC claimed it targeted U.S. bases in Kuwait and infrastructure in Bahrain.

The Pentagon confirmed that 18 U.S. service members have been killed since the conflict escalated, with a Pentagon spokesman also revealing that 100 additional service members were injured in the past two weeks alone.
sections.whyItMatters: A strike on Pickaxe Mountain would be a qualitative escalation from tactical strikes to targeting Iran's most fortified nuclear facility. It would also sharpen the contradiction: vowing to destroy Iran's nuclear program while enabling Saudi enrichment the same week.

sections.heading: Houthi Blockade Forces Saudi Tankers to Reverse in Red Sea
sections.body:
Yemen's Houthi movement declared a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, warning shipping companies they would target vessels loading or unloading Saudi crude. The immediate effect: three oil tankers carrying Saudi crude for China and India reversed course in the Red Sea after the Houthi warning, according to MarineTraffic and Reuters reporting by Florence Tan and Nidhi Verma.

Asian refiners are now seeking to reroute Saudi crude through the Suez Canal and around the Cape of Good Hope, adding significant transit time and cost. The Bab el-Mandeb Strait is the only sea route from the Red Sea to the Indian Ocean, and it has become even more critical for Saudi Arabia since the Strait of Hormuz was effectively closed by the U.S.-Iran conflict. Saudi Arabia had been shipping approximately 4 million barrels per day through Yanbu and Red Sea ports — up from about 1 million bpd before the war — with about 2.5 million bpd heading south through Bab el-Mandeb to Asian buyers.

Saudi Arabia condemned the Houthi blockade, calling it a threat to global energy supplies. The Houthis responded by calling the Saudi regime 'criminal.' President Trump stated the Houthis had not yet closed Bab el-Mandeb but warned the U.S. would respond if they attempted to block the waterway.
sections.whyItMatters: With Hormuz already disrupted, the Houthi threat to Bab el-Mandeb effectively closes both of Saudi Arabia's main sea export routes. This is a supply chokepoint crisis compounding on itself — and there is no quick pipeline fix available.

sections.heading: Brent Surges Past $91 as Refined Product Crisis Deepens
sections.body:
Oil prices continued their sharp climb, with Brent crude trading above $91 per barrel on Wednesday — a five-week high — as dual chokepoint disruptions and escalating U.S.-Iran strikes drove supply fears. According to the Sydney Morning Herald's analysis citing IEA data, about three-quarters of the 400 million barrels released from strategic reserves since March have been drawn down, and remaining reserves could be exhausted within weeks.

The bigger crisis is in refined products. U.S. diesel stocks are at 20-year lows and gasoline stocks at their lowest since 2012, the SMH analysis noted. Global refining output is about 5 million barrels per day lower than a year ago, due to Iranian attacks on Middle East refineries, Ukraine's strikes on Russian refining infrastructure, and Russia's diesel export ban. U.S. gasoline prices have risen back above $4 per gallon (from $3.14 a year ago), with diesel at about $5.14 per gallon.

The IEA's July oil market report noted that OECD and non-OECD oil stocks fell by a combined roughly 100 million barrels last month, even during the brief ceasefire period. The ceasefire had temporarily boosted crude flows to about 75% of prewar levels, but refined product exports from the Persian Gulf remained less than half of prewar levels.
sections.whyItMatters: The refined product squeeze is now the acute threat to the global economy — not just crude supply. Strategic reserves are nearly depleted, and with two major chokepoints threatened simultaneously, the remaining buffers are running thin ahead of the U.S. midterm elections.

sections.heading: Russia Strikes Ukraine-Bound LPG Tanker in Romanian Waters
sections.body:
A Liberia-flagged LPG tanker, the Gas Lisbon, carrying liquefied petroleum gas from Egypt's Alexandria to the Ukrainian port of Reni, was struck off Romania's Black Sea coast on Monday. Romanian President Nicusor Dan confirmed the attack occurred approximately 20 nautical miles off the Romanian coast in international waters, wounding three crew members.

President Dan said the attack was 'most likely part of the Russian Federation's illegal war of aggression against Ukraine.' Acting Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha called it 'yet another example of Russia's terror.' The strike follows a pattern of escalating Russian attacks on commercial shipping near Ukraine: a Russian missile strike on a Turkish-owned cargo ship departing Odesa on Sunday killed 10 people, and a July 14 strike killed the captain of a Tanzania-flagged vessel.

Ukraine has also been targeting Russia's shadow fleet, saying it struck 116 shadow fleet tankers within 9 days earlier this month, arguing the vessels bankroll Moscow's war machine and constitute lawful military targets.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia is expanding its targeting of energy shipping to NATO-adjacent waters. A strike on a commercial LPG tanker 20 nautical miles off Romania's coast — near NATO territory — represents a direct challenge to the alliance's security guarantees and to Black Sea energy logistics.

sections.heading: ADNOC Sanctions $6.2 Billion Umm Shaif Gas Project Amid Supply Crunch
sections.body:
Abu Dhabi's ADNOC has taken a final investment decision on the $6.2 billion Umm Shaif Gas Cap project, aimed at producing 600 million standard cubic feet of natural gas per day — roughly 10% of the UAE's daily domestic gas consumption. The project will be developed in partnership with TotalEnergies, Eni, and China National Petroleum Corporation (CNPC), with Dh18.8 billion in offshore EPC contracts awarded to consortia of Emirati and international firms.

ADNOC also launched a global LNG marketing and trading platform in the Abu Dhabi Global Market, targeting 47 million tons per year of LNG by 2035. ADNOC CEO Dr. Sultan Ahmed Al Jaber said the company is 'accelerating the implementation of our integrated gas strategy' to strengthen the UAE's global LNG position.

Separately, Egypt is in talks with Shell, TotalEnergies, and BP for a multi-year deal to purchase 15-18 LNG cargoes per month for at least three years, according to Reuters — a stark signal of how the Gulf conflict has upended even non-Gulf energy flows. Egypt had been an LNG exporter before the war; it now needs imports to cover domestic shortfalls.
sections.whyItMatters: The UAE is pushing massive gas investment precisely when its sea export routes are under threat. The inclusion of CNPC as a partner signals continued Chinese involvement in Gulf energy projects despite geopolitical tensions. Egypt's shift from LNG exporter to importer shows how far the conflict's energy disruption extends.

sections.heading: Trump Order Targets China-Linked Critical Mineral Supply Chains for Defense
sections.body:
President Trump signed an executive order requiring U.S. defense contractors to identify and reduce dependence on China-linked critical mineral sources, targeting the military supply chain specifically. The order mandates that defense firms trace the origin of minerals like rare earths, lithium, and cobalt used in weapons systems, electronics, and energy storage, and develop plans to cut reliance on Beijing-linked suppliers.

The move follows earlier Trump administration actions on critical minerals, including the Interior Department's proposal to lease over 31 million acres off American Samoa for seabed mining, part of a broader push to boost domestic critical minerals production. Mining stocks including Critical Metals Corp (CRML), MP Materials, and USA Rare Earth saw premarket gains on the news.

The G7 agreed in June that no single country should supply more than 60% of rare earth imports by 2030, but achieving that goal requires permitting reform, according to U.S. mining lawyers cited by Mining.com. Battery recycling companies are also positioning themselves as faster alternatives to new mining, with firms like ReElement Technologies rapidly expanding workforce in Indiana.
sections.whyItMatters: The order is the first U.S. mandate specifically targeting defense contractors' mineral supply chains — a narrower but more enforceable approach than economy-wide decoupling. China controls roughly 60-70% of global rare earth processing, making this a genuine vulnerability for military systems from missiles to F-35s.

bottomLine: The energy geopolitics landscape deteriorated on multiple fronts simultaneously. The Trump administration approved a Saudi nuclear deal that could allow domestic uranium enrichment without the IAEA's strongest inspection protocol — the same week Trump threatened to strike Iran's most fortified nuclear facility, a contradiction that will not be lost on Gulf states or nonproliferation advocates. The Houthi blockade of Bab el-Mandeb, combined with the already-disrupted Hormuz, now threatens both of Saudi Arabia's main sea export routes, forcing tankers to reverse course and adding a second chokepoint crisis on top of the first. With strategic reserves nearly exhausted, refined product stocks at multi-decade lows, and Brent above $91, the market's remaining buffers are thin. Russia's strike on a commercial LPG tanker near Romanian waters marks another escalation in Black Sea energy shipping warfare. The U.S.-Iran conflict has entered its most intense phase — and the real energy shock may still be ahead.
sources: AP, Reuters, Straits Times, Sydney Morning Herald, Kyiv Post, NPR, Khaleej Times, EconoTimes, News18, Kurdistan24, Mining.com, Forbes, Yahoo Finance, MarineTraffic, Indian Express, SCMP, Upstream Online, Reuters Energy Watch
